nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, the Jaguar Catfish inhabits soft, acidic, low-conductivity blackwater rivers typical of the Rio Negro tributaries and Trombetas drainage in Brazil, sheltering among submerged roots, driftwood tangles, and beds of leaf litter.

decorPreferences: large pieces of driftwood and tangled root structures for shelter
overhanging caves or hollow logs for daytime refuge
leaf litter (Indian almond/catappa leaves) to replicate blackwater biotope
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
fine dark substrate (sand or fine gravel)

diet.notes: In the wild, the Jaguar Catfish is a predator of small fish and invertebrates. In the aquarium it readily accepts meaty frozen foods such as bloodworm, earthworm, prawn, mussel, and chopped fish flesh; larger specimens will also take whole prawns. Sinking pellets and carnivore sticks are accepted by established fish. Feeding should take place at lights-out to coincide with the fish's natural nocturnal activity. Dry foods alone are not adequate; rotating through several frozen protein sources provides better long-term nutrition.

goodWith: large peaceful cichlids (severum, geophagus) that occupy mid-water
large South American tetras (silver dollars, large-bodied characins)
armoured catfish of similar size (large pleco species)
other robust bottom-dwellers of comparable size
avoidWith: small fish that fit in its mouth (neon tetras, small rasboras, pygmy corydoras)
dwarf shrimp and small invertebrates — will be eaten
boisterous or aggressive tankmates that may outcompete it at feeding
fish requiring bright, active lighting conditions

imageCandidates.notes: 19th-century scientific illustration from Schomburgk (1852), Fishes of British Guiana. Public domain via University of Washington Freshwater and Marine Image Bank (accession #38613). Historical illustration quality; original species name Arius oncinus.

summary: The Jaguar Catfish (Liosomadoras oncinus) is a boldly patterned blackwater catfish from the Rio Negro basin of Brazil. Named for its spotted, jaguar-like coat, this secretive nocturnal predator grows to around 17 cm and belongs to the family Auchenipteridae, notable for internal fertilisation.
careNotes: The Jaguar Catfish is one of the most visually striking members of the driftwood catfish family Auchenipteridae, adorned with a bold dark-spotted or blotched pattern on a pale background that recalls a jaguar’s coat. In the wild it inhabits the soft, acidic blackwaters of the Rio Negro basin (including the Branco River tributary) and the Trombetas River drainage in Brazil, sheltering within submerged root masses, driftwood tangles, and leaf litter during the day. A tank of at least 200 L with a 120 cm footprint is recommended; the aquarium must include substantial driftwood architecture providing cave-like sheltering spots, as this fish is strictly nocturnal and will spend daylight hours hidden. Dim lighting — achieved through floating plants or subdued room lighting — reduces stress. Water quality should replicate the blackwater biotope: soft and low in hardness, low in alkalinity, and mildly acidic to neutral, with a pH of 5.0 to 7.0 and a temperature of 22 to 26°C. Tannin-stained water produced by Indian almond leaves or peat is beneficial and closely mirrors wild conditions. As a member of the Auchenipteridae, which tends to be lightly armoured rather than fully scaled, this species is sensitive to copper-based medications, and any copper treatment should be avoided. Feed after lights-out with meaty frozen foods such as bloodworm, prawn, earthworm, and chopped fish. Despite a tendency toward aggression with very small fish, the Jaguar Catfish is generally peaceful toward tankmates too large to swallow. Provide a tight-fitting lid, as escape risk is considered moderate for larger catfish.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely reported and considered difficult. Auchenipteridae are notable among catfishes for internal fertilisation: the male possesses a modified, intromittent anal fin (an andropodium) used to inseminate the female, who subsequently stores sperm and can fertilise multiple clutches. Females deposit adhesive eggs in sheltered sites; no extended parental care is documented. Successful captive spawning would require a dedicated species or small-group setup, very soft acidic blackwater, and provision of enclosed spawning cavities within driftwood. Due to the rarity of captive breeding records, this species is generally obtained as wild-caught.

primary.name: FishBase — Liosomadoras oncinus
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed max size 17.0 cm TL (male/unsexed), temperature 20–24°C, pH 4.8–6.8, hardness 0–12 dH, native range (Branco River basin, Brazil), family Auchenipteridae, order Siluriformes, trophic level 3.4 (carnivore), IUCN Least Concern (assessed 13 August 2020), aquarium trade commercial use. Temperature upper range conservatively extended to 26°C per general Amazonian blackwater practice; FishBase lower bound 20°C retained and raised to 22°C per typical blackwater catfish husbandry.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Jaguar catfish
additional.notes: Confirmed Auchenipteridae family, endemic to Brazil in the Branco River and Trombetas River, aquarium trade presence, IUCN Least Concern. Added Trombetas River to native range.

additional.name: Wikimedia Commons — FMIB 38613 Arius oncinus
additional.notes: Public-domain scientific illustration (Schomburgk 1852). Original combination Arius oncinus = current valid name Liosomadoras oncinus. Used as imageCandidates primary recommendation.

additional.name: FishBase — Liosomadoras oncinus synonyms
additional.notes: Confirmed valid synonyms: Arius oncinus Jardine, 1841 (original combination); Lisomadoras oncinus (misspelling, invalid). Taxonomic author and year confirmed: (Jardine, 1841).

fish.breedingNotes: The Jaguar Catfish is rarely if ever bred in home aquaria. Internal fertilisation via the male's andropodium (a modified anal fin) is an uncommon trait among catfishes and sets the Auchenipteridae apart from most other catfish families. Wild-caught stock makes up the great majority of fish available in the hobby. Any serious breeding attempt would require a dedicated species setup with very soft, acidic blackwater and enclosed spawning cavities within driftwood.
